coredata.dc:title: Ethanol-sensitive nearly aligned ZnO nanorod thin films covered by graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Rahimi, Kourosh
Yazdani, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were simply synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid, and zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NRs) were grown on pre-seeded glass substrates by a solvothermal method. Finally, the GQDs were dropped on the ZnO NR thin film. It was found that the incorporation of GQDs eliminated the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the ZnO NR thin film, suggesting that electrons of ZnO can be readily transferred to GQDs, contributing to the passing current. Finally, the ethanol sensing analysis was performed, showing that the incorporation of GQDs enhanced the sensitivity of ZnO NR thin film to ethanol gas, and the mechanism behind the enhancement was discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensors
Thin films
Zinc oxide nanorod
Graphene quantum dot

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM image and XRD pattern (inset) of the bare ZnO NR thin film.
AFM micrograph of GQDs. The height profile of a line is also shown. Photographs of the synthesized GQDs suspension under visible light and 370 nm UVA lamp.
FTIR spectra of the bare ZnO NR thin film and GQD-ZnO NR thin film. Photoluminescence spectra of the thin films, excited by 325 nm, are also shown in the inset.
Transient sensitivity plots of the bare ZnO NR thin film and the GQD-ZnO NR thin film.

head.ce:title: Ethanol-sensitive nearly aligned ZnO nanorod thin films covered by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Condensed Matter Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were simply synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid, and zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NRs) were grown on pre-seeded glass substrates by a solvothermal method. Finally, the GQDs were dropped on the ZnO NR thin film. It was found that the incorporation of GQDs eliminated the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the ZnO NR thin film, suggesting that electrons of ZnO can be readily transferred to GQDs, contributing to the passing current. Finally, the ethanol sensing analysis was performed, showing that the incorporation of GQDs enhanced the sensitivity of ZnO NR thin film to ethanol gas, and the mechanism behind the enhancement was discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal oxides show considerable potentials in gas sensing. ZnO, as a wide and direct bandgap (3.37 eV) semiconductor, has been extensively explored for gas sensing applications, because it has a good response to various reducing and oxidizing gases, it is inexpensive, it has a good chemical and physical stability under the operating conditions of sensors, and it is environmental friendly. ZnO nanorods are attractive for gas sensing applications due to their large surface area/volume ratio, leading to numerous active sites to participate in reactions with gas molecules, and their preferred longitudinal charge transportation because of their shape [1].

Graphene can provide a very high electron mobility for ZnO nanorods. However, when it comes to gas sensing applications, the huge surface of graphene can prevent the target gas molecules from reaching to ZnO nanorods. Graphene quantum dots can solve the problem because of their tiny lateral size that can penetrate into the pores while having a relatively high electron mobility. GQDs can provide more interconnectivity among ZnO nanorods to provide channels for transferring electrons. Moreover, GQDs can provide numerous active sites, due to their defective structure and functional groups, to promote the adsorption of gas molecules. Furthermore, they have a semiconducting nature [2], so they do not make the ZnO NRs a conductor, which is unsuitable for sensing applications.

Here, GQDs were simply dropped on ZnO NR thin films to enhance their ethanol sensing properties. To the best of our knowledge, no study has been reported on the enhancement of gas sensing properties of ZnO nanorod thin film by using graphene quantum dot; however, the acetic gas sensing properties of GQD-ZnO powder have been recently studied [3]. Finally, the mechanism behind the enhancement was discussed.

We have described the synthesis and characterization of GQDs elsewhere [4]. In summary, 2 g citric acid was heated at 200 °C for 30 min, and the product pH was increased to 7 by adding an aqueous NaOH solution (10 mg/mL). To prepare the ZnO NR thin film, at first, 0.05 M zinc acetate dehydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O) and 0.06 M diethanolamine (HN(CH2CH2OH)2) was prepared in ethanol, and it was heated at 60 °C while stirring for 30 min to become clear. ZnO nanoparticles as seed were spin-coated on glass substrates, and the substrates were dried at 200 °C for 10 min, and the procedure was repeated for totally five times. The substrates were annealed at 500 °C for one hour in air. The pre-seeded substrates were suspended in an aqueous solution containing 0.001 M zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) at 70 °C for 90 min. Finally, the substrates were annealed at 500 °C for 1 h. GQDs were simply dropped on the ZnO NR thin film, and it was dried at 300 °C for 1 h.

A home-built instrument (consisting of a heating holder, a fan, and a voltmeter) with a known volume was designed to perform the sensing analysis, by injecting a specific amount of ethanol and monitoring the changes in the voltage of a resistor connected in series. The gas sensing tests were performed with 500-ppm ethanol and with the samples preheated to 300 °C. A 5 V bias was applied to the circuit.

The following instruments were employed for characterizations: XRD (X’Pert MPD, Philips), FESEM (Hitachi S-4160), AFM (Veeco Autoprobe CP-research), PL spectrophotometer (Gilden pλotonics), and FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet 680).

A FESEM image of the ZnO NR thin film is shown in Fig. 1 . The nanorods are almost vertical, with an approximate diameter of ∼60 nm. The XRD pattern of the thin film is also shown in the inset of Fig. 1. Diffraction peaks were matched with a hexagonal wurtzite phase, corresponding to JCPDS Card No. 01-079-0208. The sharp main peak (002) indicates the high crystallinity and the c-axis preferred growth direction of the ZnO wurtzite nanorods. Moreover, there is no secondary phase or any significant impurity, since no other peak is observed.

Fig. 2 shows a AFM micrograph of the prepared GQDs on a mica substrate. The GQDs are mostly nanosheets of 1–1.5 nm in thickness and 10–40 nm in diameter. Photographs of the synthesized GQDs suspension under visible light and UV 370 nm light are also shown in the inset of Fig. 2. As it is seen, GQDs emit a greenish blue color when excited by a UVA lamp.

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the bare ZnO NR and GQD-ZnO NR thin films. The band around 430 cm−1 in both spectra is due to the stretching vibration of Zn–O bonds in ZnO [5]. The band around 3336 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching mode of O–H bonds in the hydroxyl groups [6]. The shoulder about 2981 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–H bonds [7]. The band around 1619 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CC bonds in sp2 carbon, and the band around 1388 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C–O bonds [8]. The photoluminescence spectra of the thin films, excited by the wavelength of 325 nm, are also shown in the inset of Fig. 3. The ultraviolet emission at 386 nm results from the recombination of the generated electrons and holes, which is the characteristic emission of ZnO. Moreover, a weak peak is observed at 462 nm which corresponds to the existing structural defects, like impurities and vacancies. The interesting point is that the incorporation of GQD eliminated the peaks. This is because the GQDs have a good contact with ZnO NRs, and can trap the electrons of ZnO.

Fig. 4 shows the transient sensitivity (S = (Ig − I0)/I0) of the bare ZnO NR thin film and the GQD-ZnO NR thin film for three successive cycles for 500 ppm ethanol gas. The sensitivity of the GQD-ZnO NR thin film is significantly higher than that of the bare ZnO NR thin film. The maximum sensitivity of the bare ZnO NR thin film slightly increases as the number of sensing cycle increases; however, it is seen that the maximum sensitivity of the GQD-ZnO NR thin film slightly decreases, and reaches a slightly uniform state.

The mechanism of gas sensing in ZnO is based on the changes in its electrical properties in the presence of a target gas. As a naturally n-type semiconductor (due to the naturally existing oxygen vacancies or zinc interstitials [9]), ZnO gives its electrons to oxygen molecules (O2) when exposed to the ambient air, due to the strong electronegativity of oxygen. Thus, some oxygen radicals such as O2−, O−, and O2 − are generated on ZnO surfaces; however, O− species are dominant in the middle temperature [10]. The reaction is as follows:
O 2
+
e
-
ZnO
is
n
- type
→
O
2
-
O
2
-
+
e
-
→ 2
O
-

The phenomenon leads to the creation of a high resistivity depletion layer near the surface. Therefore, ZnO shows a higher resistivity in the presence of the ambient air compared to its resistivity in the vacuum. As a reductive gas, ethanol molecules could be chemisorbed on ZnO surfaces to react with the adsorbed oxygen species, forming H2O and CO2, and subsequently releasing the electrons back to the ZnO conduction band, so the reaction can be written as follows [11]:
Ethanol
C 2
H 5
OH
+
O
-
→ C
H 3
CHO +
H 2
O +
e
-
C
H 3
CHO + 5
O
-
→ 2 C
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O + 5
e
-

Subsequently, the generated CO2 and H2O molecules desorb from ZnO surfaces, and the released electrons return to their initial band, leading to a higher electron concentration. Therefore, ethanol can decrease the resistivity of ZnO. The phenomenon is utilized for ethanol gas sensing.

GQDs play two major roles: (1) GQDs could promote the adsorption of oxygen and gas molecules, and (2) GQDs could provide a path to transfer electrons by providing interconnections among ZnO nanorods, and due to their high electron mobility, they can facilitate the transport of charge carriers. Moreover, GQDs do not suffer from a disadvantage of graphene: that is, the very wide area of graphene, which may prevent the target gases from reaching to the ZnO NRs. GQDs, with their little lateral size, can readily penetrate between the ZnO NRs.

In summary, GQDs were dropped on ZnO NR thin films. The incorporation of GQDs significantly enhanced the sensitivity of ZnO NR thin film to ethanol gas. Finally, the mechanism behind the enhancement was discussed. The paper can pave the way toward using GQDs for enhancing gas sensing properties of metal oxides.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.
